medications.indication_rationale: A short course of an oral NSAID targets the superficial venous inflammation and pain of superficial thrombophlebitis and is more effective than paracetamol here. It should be arranged with a clinician who confirms no contraindications (GI, renal, cardiovascular, anticoagulant interaction) and, in extensive superficial thrombophlebitis near the sapheno-femoral junction, considers prophylactic anticoagulation instead of / in addition to NSAIDs. The source prescribed ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with food.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 400 mg orally three times daily with food (as prescribed in the source in-person encounter; confirm no NSAID contraindication at assessment)
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no peptic ulcer/GI bleeding risk, renal impairment, uncontrolled hypertension/cardiovascular contraindication, or anticoagulant use before recommending an NSAID
Take with food; use the shortest effective course

medications.indication_rationale: The patient was already using over-the-counter paracetamol with minimal relief for the inflammatory pain; it may be continued for background analgesia but an NSAID is the preferred anti-inflammatory for the inflamed vein if not contraindicated.
contraindications_in_this_case: Provided minimal relief for this inflammatory pain; do not rely on it alone if an NSAID is appropriate

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Warm compress to the affected area
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: A warm compress can ease the discomfort of the inflamed superficial vein as a supportive measure alongside NSAIDs and elevation.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Forcing a tight compression stocking over the acutely inflamed painful cord
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Tight compression over the acutely inflamed area worsens pain; properly fitted graduated compression is valuable for the underlying varicose veins once the acute inflammation settles.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Elevate the legs above the level of the heart for about 20 minutes each evening (and when resting), and watch the hard red area for spread or new whole-leg swelling.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation uses gravity to drain static venous blood, reducing venous pressure and the heavy aching, while monitoring detects propagation early.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Immediately stop wearing tight socks with constricting elastic bands, and reduce prolonged uninterrupted standing at work (take breaks, walk, elevate when able).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Constricting bands impede venous return and prolonged standing worsens venous pooling and disease progression.

patient_education_points.point: One of your bulging surface veins has developed a small clot with inflammation (superficial thrombophlebitis) - that is the hard, red, painful cord. This is painful but usually NOT the dangerous deep type of clot. Because it is near the deep veins, it should be examined in person promptly (today or within a day or two) to confirm it, rule out a deep clot, and check your pulses.

An anti-inflammatory medicine (like ibuprofen) usually settles the pain and redness better than paracetamol, provided it is safe for you - take it with food. Keep the leg elevated and use a warm compress. Do not force a tight stocking over the sore red area until it settles, and stop wearing tight elastic-band socks.

Seek emergency care immediately if your whole calf suddenly swells up much bigger than the other leg, the redness spreads rapidly with a fever, or you develop sudden breathlessness, sharp chest pain on breathing in, or cough up blood - these can mean a deep clot or a clot that has travelled to the lungs.

Superficial thrombophlebitis usually settles over a few weeks with anti-inflammatories. Your underlying varicose veins can be assessed with an ultrasound scan and treated electively later - minor procedures can treat the faulty veins.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt in-person assessment (urgent, same day to within 24-48 hours) to confirm superficial thrombophlebitis and exclude DVT; routine GP review within 1 week to ensure it is resolving and to arrange a venous duplex ultrasound
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of the inflamed cord, exclusion of DVT, tolerance of the NSAID, planning of a venous duplex ultrasound, and elective management of the underlying varicose veins and compression once settled
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Whole-leg swelling, spreading redness with fever, or any breathlessness/pleuritic chest pain/haemoptysis - seek emergency care immediately.

must_include_items: Recognition of acute superficial thrombophlebitis complicating chronic varicose veins
Prompt in-person assessment with active DVT exclusion (the important-not-to-miss)
An anti-inflammatory (NSAID) as first-line for the inflamed vein if not contraindicated, with paracetamol acknowledged as insufficient
Supportive measures: elevation, warm compress, stop tight elastic-band socks; defer tight compression over the acute area
Robust safety-netting for DVT (whole-leg swelling), cellulitis (spreading redness/fever) and PE (breathlessness/pleuritic chest pain/haemoptysis)
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day or within 24-48 hour in-person assessment are acceptable given systemic wellness; 'inflamed surface-vein clot' / 'superficial thrombophlebitis' are acceptable framings
Arranging venous duplex ultrasound urgently or semi-urgently are both acceptable provided DVT is excluded
Considering prophylactic anticoagulation for extensive superficial thrombophlebitis near the sapheno-femoral junction is an acceptable clinician-led escalation
errors_of_omission: Not recognising the acute superficial thrombophlebitis (dismissing it as the patient's usual varicose veins)
No DVT exclusion / no arrangement for in-person assessment or ultrasound
Leaving the patient on paracetamol alone without considering an anti-inflammatory
errors_of_commission: Over-triage: sending a stable, systemically-well patient to ED by ambulance for a localised superficial process
Advising a tight compression stocking over the acutely inflamed area
Recommending an NSAID without any check for contraindications
Reassuring it is 'just your varicose veins' with no assessment or safety-netting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (1) recognising superficial thrombophlebitis on varicose veins and arranging prompt in-person assessment with DVT exclusion, (2) appropriate anti-inflammatory/supportive management without harmful tight compression over the acute area, and (3) robust DVT/PE/cellulitis safety-netting. Both under-triage (dismissing as usual varicose veins, missing a DVT) and gross over-triage (emergency ambulance for a stable localised process) are failures.
